title: Parallax Compatibility Case (M12)
description: The case envelope. goldenSql is keyed by dialect from day one so the compatibility matrix grows without a redesign. A dialect's golden SQL is EITHER a single statement (single-statement read cases, Phases 1-2) OR an ordered list of statements — one per relationship/deep-fetch level (deep-fetch cases, Phase 3+), one per milestone-chaining DML step (writeSequence cases, Phase 5), or one per batched-write step (writeSequence cases, Phase 6). A case is one of seven shapes: a read case (carries an `operation`, asserts `expectedRows`/`expectedGraph`); a writeSequence case (carries a `writeSequence`, applies its ordered DML golden SQL, then asserts `expectedTableState` — and, for the M9 detached merge-back cases, MAY first load the model's fixtures via `loadFixtures`); a scenario case (Phase 6, M8 — carries a `scenario` of ordered operation steps with per-step round-trip counts + identity/cache assertions, whose declared counts MUST be internally consistent with the listed golden SQL); a conflict case (Phase 7, M10 — carries `expectedAffectedRows`, optionally an out-of-band `precondition`, and a golden `UPDATE`, asserting the affected-row count an optimistic-lock UPDATE leaves behind); a coherence case (Phase 11, cross-process cache coherence — carries a `coherence` two-node operation sequence run over two connections to one database, asserting node B observes node A's committed write rather than a stale cached copy); an **error case** (carries `errorClass` + `expectedNativeCode`, M11 error-code classification — triggers a real DB error, single-connection for `uniqueViolation` or a two-connection `concurrency` choreography for `deadlock`/`lockWaitTimeout`, and asserts the neutral category the error classifies to plus the per-dialect native code); or a **boundary case** (M8/M10 bounded automatic retry — carries `boundary` ordered actions + `expect` portable outcome, optionally an injected fault `inject`, declaring the unit-of-work loop mechanics the single-connection M12 harness cannot provoke). Every case carries an optional `lane` (`harness` default | `api-conformance`): a `harness`-lane case executes as today, while an `api-conformance`-lane case (every boundary case, plus the read-lock matrix reads) is schema-validated by the harness but satisfied by each language's API Conformance Suite.

oneOf.description: A read case: a queryable operation with expected rows or an expected object graph.

A writeSequence case: ordered DML asserted by the resulting table state (milestone-chaining temporal writes, Phase 5; batched non-temporal writes, Phase 6; M9 detached insert/update merge-back, Phase 7).
A scenario case (M8): ordered operation steps with per-step round-trip counts proving the cache/identity contract. Golden SQL lives per step, so the top-level goldenSql is absent.

A conflict case (M10): an optimistic-lock UPDATE asserted by the number of rows it affects. Loads the model's fixtures, OPTIONALLY runs an out-of-band `precondition` (e.g. a stale-version mutation by a concurrent transaction), then runs the golden UPDATE(s) and asserts the affected-row count. Two forms: a SINGLE attempt (`expectedAffectedRows` + `goldenSql`); or an ordered `attempts` retry sequence (each attempt carries its own goldenSql + expectedAffectedRows), which proves the M10 retry contract — a stale UPDATE affects 0 rows, then a retry that re-reads the fresh version affects 1.

A coherence case (Phase 11, cross-process cache coherence): a two-node operation sequence run against ONE database over TWO connections (node A, node B). Each step declares its node and golden SQL; the case asserts what node B observes after node A's committed write (the new state, never a stale cached copy). The harness provisions one database, opens two connections, runs each step on its node, and asserts the final re-fetch returns the post-write rows.

An error-classification case (M11): triggers a real DB error and asserts the neutral category it classifies to (errorClass) plus the per-dialect native code (expectedNativeCode). A case MUST declare a trigger: top-level `goldenSql` (single-connection, uniqueViolation — ordered DML whose final statement raises) OR a `concurrency` choreography (two-connection, deadlock / lockWaitTimeout — barrier-separated rounds).

A boundary case (M8/M10 bounded automatic retry): a unit-of-work loop-mechanics case the single-connection M12 harness cannot provoke (injected transient failures, retry counting, error surfacing). It carries `lane: api-conformance` and is satisfied by each language's API Conformance Suite; the harness schema-validates it but does not execute it. It declares the portable unit-of-work body (`boundary` ordered actions), the portable expected outcome (`expect`: `committed` or a surfaced error kind), an OPTIONAL injected fault (`inject`), and its retry configuration under `uow` (`retries` / `retryOptimisticConflicts`). It carries no golden SQL — the concrete DML and error types stay per-language. Both invariants are enforced here: `lane` is REQUIRED and pinned to `api-conformance` (a boundary case cannot silently default to the `harness` lane and fall into compile/run paths not shaped for it), and `goldenSql` is disallowed.

model.description: Path to the model descriptor, relative to core/compatibility/.

tags.description: Module/feature tags driving coverage and test selection.

lane.description: Which executor satisfies this case (M12 lane routing). `harness` (the default) — the M12 compatibility harness executes it as today (golden-SQL / data observables). `api-conformance` — a runtime-loop or read-lock-matrix branch a single-connection harness cannot provoke (injected transient failures, retry counting, error surfacing, or the emitted read-lock proof): the harness schema-validates it but does NOT execute it, and each language's API Conformance Suite MUST satisfy it (coverage enforced the way `covered.ts` self-asserts). Every `boundary`-shape case is `api-conformance` by construction; the read-lock matrix reads (`0616`-`0619`) are `read`-shape `api-conformance` cases.

uow.description: The unit-of-work configuration a case runs under (M8 strategy selection + M8/M10 bounded automatic retry). Optional and descriptive: it declares the per-unit-of-work concurrency mode (so a case's mode-dependent golden SQL is self-describing) and the retry configuration a boundary case exercises. `concurrency`: `locking` — the default M8 implicit shared read lock, keyed updates advance the version with NO gate; `optimistic` — reads take no lock and every keyed update / temporal close GATES on the observed version. The harness executes the authored golden SQL either way; the block documents WHICH mode produced it. The retry keys drive a boundary case's api-conformance loop.

concurrency.description: The correctness strategy this unit of work selects: `locking` (default — M8 implicit shared read lock; version advances but no gate is emitted) or `optimistic` (M10 — reads take no lock; keyed updates and temporal closes gate on the observed version).

retries.description: The bound on automatic re-executions of the unit-of-work body after a retriable failure (M8/M10 bounded automatic retry). Default 10; `0` disables the loop, so even a transient failure surfaces to the caller.

retryOptimisticConflicts.description: Whether an optimistic-lock conflict joins the retriable set for this unit of work (M10). Default false — a conflict surfaces to the caller after one attempt. Transient database failures (deadlock / serialization failure) are always retriable regardless of this flag.

boundary.description: boundary cases only (M8/M10 bounded automatic retry). An ordered list of the actions the unit-of-work body performs, portably described so the case is self-documenting without carrying golden SQL. The M12 harness never executes these (the case is `api-conformance`-lane); each language's suite realizes them through its idiomatic public API.

action.description: The unit-of-work action this step performs, portably named (the concrete API is per-language).

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the action's role in the loop scenario.

inject.description: boundary cases only. A portable fault kind injected at the database-port seam to drive the retry loop (aligned with the M11 `errorClass` vocabulary): a `serialization-failure` or `deadlock` transient (always retriable), a `lock-wait-timeout` (never retriable), or an `optimistic-lock-conflict` (retriable only under `uow.retryOptimisticConflicts`). Absent when the case exercises only loop configuration (`retries: 0`, the callback-withheld-on-abort case).

expect.description: boundary cases only. The portable expected outcome: `committed` (the unit of work eventually commits — the injected fault was auto-retried away), `aborted` (the closure threw and the boundary rolled back, withholding the callback's return value — the M8 abort contract / ADR 0008), or a surfaced error kind the caller observes (`optimistic-lock-conflict` when a conflict is not opted into retry; `deadlock` / `serialization-failure` / `lock-wait-timeout` when a transient exhausts the bound or the loop is disabled). The concrete per-language error type (e.g. `ParallaxOptimisticLockError`) stays language-local; this is the neutral kind the suite asserts.

operation.description: A canonical M2 algebra node (validated separately against operation.schema.json). Present on read cases; absent on writeSequence cases.

writeSequence.description: A writeSequence case: an ordered list of write steps the goldenSql DML realizes. Each step names a mutation kind on an entity; the goldenSql for the dialect is the matching ordered list of DML statements (one or more per step), applied in order, after which the harness asserts `expectedTableState`. The list documents the intent the DML must realize and lets the harness assert step count consistency. Used by milestone-chaining temporal writes (Phase 5, M7 — insert/update/terminate), batched non-temporal writes (Phase 6, M8 — a multi-row insert / a batched update), detached merge-back delete (M9), and the minimal dependent cascade-delete witness (M5/M8).

mutation.description: The mutation kind. insert / update / terminate are the M7 audit-only mutation surface and also cover the M8 batched non-temporal writes; delete covers ordinary non-temporal delete / detached-delete merge-back; cascadeDelete is the minimal dependent-delete witness over M1 dependent relationships; insertUntil / updateUntil / terminateUntil are the full-bitemporal `*Until` trio (DQ11, Phase 8) that bound a mutation to a business window — updateUntil/terminateUntil drive the rectangle split (inactivate the original on the processing axis, chain head/middle/tail business rows at fresh processing time).

entity.description: The temporal entity the mutation targets.

statements.description: How many goldenSql DML statements this step emits (for documentation / count consistency). The sum over all steps MUST equal the number of goldenSql statements per dialect.

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the milestone-chaining effect.

scenario.description: A scenario (Phase 6, M8) case: an ordered list of steps proving the unit-of-work / identity / query-cache contract. A step is either a READ step (issues a `find`) or a WRITE step (commits DML via `write`). Golden SQL lives PER STEP (not at the top level): each find costs a declared number of database round trips, and an interleaved committed write lets a later step prove read-your-own-writes (a dependent find observes the buffered write after the unit of work flushes) or query-cache invalidation (a find re-issued after a committed write MUST re-resolve rather than serve a stale cached row). The harness asserts the round-trip counts are internally consistent with the golden SQL each step lists (a cache-hit step lists none and declares roundTrips 0), executes the listed golden SQL against the provisioned database to confirm result-correctness, and checks identity assertions (a step may declare it returns the SAME logical object — same primary key — as an earlier step). The harness NEVER compiles an operation to SQL: the round-trip contract is proven from the fixture's own declared counts plus golden SQL.

oneOf.description: A read step: issues a find. A cache miss costs >= 1 round trip and lists golden SQL; a cache hit costs 0 and lists none.

A write step: applies golden DML between finds. Carries golden DML, not a find. By default the DML is COMMITTED so a later find observes it (read-your-own-writes / cache invalidation); with `rollback: true` the DML is applied and then ROLLED BACK, so a later find MUST observe the ORIGINAL rows (the M8 abort contract). A no-op write (a versioned UPDATE whose `set` changes no attribute — M10) issues NO DML: it declares `roundTrips: 0` and lists no `goldenSql`, mirroring a cache-hit read step.

find.description: The operation a READ step issues (a canonical M2 algebra node). Validated against operation.schema.json by the harness. Present on read steps; absent on write steps.

write.description: Marks a WRITE step and labels the mutation it applies (e.g. `insert` / `update`). The step's golden DML is applied on the unit of work's connection and, by default, COMMITTED; the step captures no rows and asserts no expectRows. Interleaving a committed write between finds proves read-your-own-writes (the unit of work flushes pending writes before a dependent find, so the find observes the write) and query-cache invalidation (a find re-issued after a committed write MUST re-resolve — modeling it as a cache hit would reuse the stale pre-write rows and fail the post-write expectRows).

rollback.description: Optional (write steps only). When true, the step's golden DML is applied and then ROLLED BACK instead of committed — the observable form of the M8 abort contract. A later find MUST re-resolve (a cache miss) and observe the ORIGINAL rows, never the aborted write; the read-your-own-writes forced flush is safe only because it lands inside the atomic scope the abort discards. The rolled-back DML still executes, so the step's roundTrips count its statements exactly as a committed write does.

roundTrips.description: How many database round trips this step costs. A READ cache HIT (an operation equal to an earlier step's) costs 0; a read cache MISS that executes one statement costs 1. A WRITE step's round trips equal the committed DML statements it lists (a no-op M10 versioned update that issues no DML costs 0 and lists no goldenSql). MUST equal the number of golden SQL statements this step lists for each dialect.

goldenSql.description: The golden SQL this step emits, keyed by dialect (single statement or ordered list). Present only for steps that hit the database (roundTrips >= 1); absent / empty for a cache hit (roundTrips 0).

binds.description: Bind values for this step's golden SQL ? placeholders (one flat list for a single statement).

expectRows.description: The rows this step's find must return against the fixture data. A cache-hit step that reuses an earlier step's result MAY restate the same expected rows.

sameObjectAs.description: Zero-based index of an earlier scenario step whose result denotes the SAME logical object(s) as this step (the identity-cache contract: same primary key => same object). The harness asserts both steps' results carry the same primary-key identity.

identityAttr.description: The result-column name carrying the primary-key identity, used to check `sameObjectAs`. Defaults to the queried entity's primary-key column.

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the step's role in the scenario.

coherence.description: A coherence (Phase 11, cross-process cache coherence) case: an ordered list of steps run against ONE database over TWO connections modeling two application servers (node A, node B). Each step names the node it runs on, the operation it issues, the golden SQL it emits per dialect, and its binds; a write step (kind `write`) is DML that COMMITS on its node, a read step (kind `read`) is a query. The final step is node B's re-fetch after node A's committed write — it carries `observeRows`, the post-write rows node B MUST observe (never the stale pre-write state). The harness runs each step on its declared connection and asserts the final re-fetch returns `observeRows`, proving the suite's post-write golden SQL is correct against real, committed cross-connection data. The harness never compiles operations to SQL and contains no cache or notification bus.

node.description: Which application-server node (which connection) this step runs on. `A` is the writer; `B` is the peer whose cache must converge on A's committed state.

kind.description: `read` issues a query (a seed read or a re-fetch); `write` issues DML that COMMITS on its node (the change a peer must observe).

find.description: The operation a `read` step issues (a canonical M2 algebra node, validated against operation.schema.json). Present on read steps; absent on write steps (which mutate rather than query).

goldenSql.description: The golden SQL this step emits, keyed by dialect (single statement or ordered list). A read step's is a SELECT; a write step's is the committing DML.

binds.description: Bind values for this step's golden SQL `?` placeholders. For a single-statement step this is one flat ordered list. For a multi-statement step it is a list-of-lists, one bind list per statement.

observeRows.description: The rows this step MUST observe. On the final node-B re-fetch this is the POST-WRITE state (proving the cache converged on node A's committed write, never the stale pre-write rows). On a seed read it is the pre-write state.

sameObjectAs.description: Zero-based index of an earlier coherence step (on the SAME node) whose observed rows denote the SAME logical object(s) as this step — the identity-preservation contract: the cross-process refresh updates the interned object in place (same primary key => same object), never forking a second object. The harness asserts both steps' rows carry the same primary-key identity; the referenced step MUST be an earlier read on the same node and both MUST observe at least one row.

identityAttr.description: The result-column name carrying the primary-key identity, used to check `sameObjectAs`. Defaults to the model root entity's primary-key column.

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the step's role in the coherence scenario.

equivalentEncodings.description: Optional alternate surface encodings of the same operation (e.g. a prefix vs a fluent spelling, or differently-ordered keys). Each MUST canonicalize to `operation` via the serde seam; the harness asserts this dialect-agnostically (layer 4c), proving precedence/serialization fidelity in the fixture itself.

goldenSql.description: Optimized SQL an implementation is expected to emit, keyed by dialect. Each dialect's value is a single statement string OR an ordered list of statement strings (one per relationship/deep-fetch level).

binds.description: Bind values for the ? placeholders in goldenSql. For a single-statement case this is one flat ordered list. For a multi-statement case it is a list-of-lists, one bind list per statement; the per-level binds for a deep-fetch child level are the distinct parent keys gathered from the previous level, which the harness asserts equal the authored list.

referenceSql.description: Independent naive oracle. Required for non-trivial cases, optional for trivial single-table predicate cases.

expectedRows.description: The rows the query must return against the fixture data (single-statement / flat-result cases).

expectedGraph.description: The assembled object graph a deep fetch must produce: the root rows, each carrying its eager-fetched related rows under the relationship name. Keyed by the root class name, whose value is a list of root objects; a related set appears under its relationship name (a list for to-many, a single object or null for to-one).

expectedTableState.description: The resulting table state a writeSequence or conflict case asserts after applying its ordered DML golden SQL. Keyed by table name; each value is the full set of rows that table must contain (order-insensitive). Row values speak DB column names; timestamp columns are authored as ISO-8601 UTC strings at core microsecond precision, with 0 through 6 fractional second digits (and the open-bound `infinity` as the literal string `infinity`).

loadFixtures.description: writeSequence cases only. When true, the model's fixtures are loaded BEFORE the ordered DML is applied — so a sequence can mutate pre-existing persisted rows (the M9 detached-update merge-back case, where the original row already exists). Defaults to false: a writeSequence case starts from an empty schema and builds its own state (the M7 milestone-chaining and M8 batched-insert cases).

precondition.description: conflict cases (M10) only. An out-of-band SQL statement (or ordered list) applied AFTER the fixtures load and BEFORE the golden UPDATE, simulating a concurrent transaction that mutated the row (e.g. bumping the optimistic-lock version) so the golden UPDATE's stale-version predicate now matches zero rows. Absent for the success case (the version is fresh, so the UPDATE affects one row).

preconditionBinds.description: conflict cases only. Bind values for the `?` placeholders in `precondition` (a flat list for a single statement, or a list-of-lists for multiple statements). Defaults to empty.

expectedAffectedRows.description: conflict cases (M10), single-attempt form. The number of rows the golden UPDATE must affect after any `precondition` is applied. A stale optimistic-lock version => 0 (conflict detected); a fresh version => 1 (success). This is the observable form of optimistic-lock conflict detection: `updatedRows != 1` is the conflict signal.

attempts.description: conflict cases (M10), retry form. An ordered list of optimistic-lock UPDATE attempts applied (after any `precondition`) in sequence, each asserting its own affected-row count — proving the M10 retry contract end-to-end: the first (stale-version) attempt affects 0 rows (the conflict signal), then a retry that re-reads the now-fresh version and re-applies affects 1. When present, the top-level single-attempt `goldenSql` / `expectedAffectedRows` are absent and `expectedTableState` (when authored) asserts the retried write landed.

goldenSql.description: This attempt's golden UPDATE, keyed by dialect.

binds.description: Bind values for this attempt's golden UPDATE ? placeholders (a flat list).

expectedAffectedRows.description: The rows this attempt's UPDATE must affect: 0 for the stale-version attempt (conflict), 1 for the fresh-version retry (success).

note.description: Optional human-readable description of this attempt's role in the retry sequence.

roundTrips.description: Declared number of SQL statements for the operation. For a deep-fetch read case this MUST equal the number of authored/executed goldenSql statements for each dialect; child SQL is omitted when an earlier level gathers no parent keys. For a writeSequence case it MUST equal the number of ordered DML statements; for a scenario case it MUST equal the SUM of the steps' per-step roundTrips (the total database cost of the scenario).

tolerance.description: Absolute tolerance for numeric row comparison. Omit for exact comparison (the default; money/counts/exact aggregates compare exactly in Decimal space). Declare ONLY for inherently inexact results (stddev/variance, repeating-decimal avg) that cannot be authored exactly and differ in scale across dialects; comparison then becomes abs(actual - expected) <= tolerance.

errorClass.description: The neutral M11 error category a triggered DB error MUST classify to. uniqueViolation = duplicate-key; deadlock = deadlock or serialization failure (retriable); lockWaitTimeout = blocked past the lock-wait budget.

expectedNativeCode.description: The native code each dialect's driver MUST surface for this error, witnessing the divergence the classifier absorbs: Postgres a SQLSTATE string (e.g. "23505"), MariaDB a vendor errno (e.g. 1062). errorClass is the single dialect-neutral seam output; this is the per-dialect input.

concurrency.description: The two-connection choreography for deadlock / lockWaitTimeout cases (absent for single-connection uniqueViolation). Ordered, barrier-separated rounds; each round names the statements nodes A and B run that round (a node absent from a round is idle). Each node step carries dialect-keyed goldenSql + binds, mirroring a coherence step. The runner runs each node on its own non-autocommit session, synchronizes rounds with a barrier, and classifies the error raised in the contention round.

oneOf.description: An ordered list of statements, one per relationship/deep-fetch level.

dialectAgnosticSql.description: Out-of-band SQL not keyed by dialect: a naive statement (or ordered list) speaking plain column names, run by the harness verbatim. Used by a conflict case's `precondition` (the same statement applies to every dialect).
